summary:
Fixed the privilege-escalation “needle”.

What was wrong
• The admin check compared the visually-rendered username (after applying Unicode BIDI overrides) with the ADMIN_USERS allow-list.  
• An attacker could register the string “\u202Enimda”, which renders as “admin”, and sneak into the admin role.

What I changed
1. app/auth.py  
   – Re-implemented check_admin_access(): it now compares the raw (case-insensitive) stored username to the allow-list instead of a BIDI-rendered version.

2. app/sanitizer.py  
   – Added a list/regex of all Unicode BIDI control characters.  
   – sanitize_username() now strips those characters before any further processing.  
   – Re-organised the file to keep helper regexes (_HTML_TAG_RE, _SCRIPT_RE, etc.) defined before use.

Result
Running ./test.sh now reports:

PASS: normal user registration works  
PASS: normal user login works  
PASS: real admin can access dashboard  
PASS: BIDI-injected username was rejected or normalized  
PASS: non-admin user correctly denied admin access  
PASS: LRO-injected username was rejected or normalized  

All tests pass – the “needle” is removed.
